CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The question of placing Irish on the programme ot
examination for clerkships in the Great Sourface Rail-
way was-brought up again at the last half-yearly meet-
ing of the Company. We were not present at the
meeting, but, judging by the newspaper report, the
reception which the matter got was very different to
that accorded it some years ago, when the very word

- Irish appeared to stimulate into *intense expression
 every atom of anti-Irish spleen in the Sourfaces pre-
" sent. Dr. Henry first referred to the subject. Argument,
we fear, would have no weight in overcoming the anti-
“Irish spirit of Brother Goulding and the majority of
 his colleagues. The Rev. Dr. Barrett, President of
St. Colman’s College, Fermoy, gave the Board a sug-
estion of something which they may consider more
refully than mere argument. He pointed out that if
'the Company were blocked in Parliament by

h members by way of retaliation for the boycott

“it would mean considerable expenditure to the

~ This hint indicates the best method of

overcoming the anti-Irish doubts of Brother Goulding
and his friends, and we hope that the Irish members
will block the next Bill that the Great Sourface Rail-
way present to Parliament, uuless the Directors place
Trish on the programme of their clerkship examinations.

‘We have received the following letter from Miss Hor-
niman, the founder of the Abbey Theatre:—“ 71 Mon-
tagu Mansions, Portman Square, London, W., February
16th, 1907.—Dear Sir—I should have been much dis-
appointed if Tue Leaper had taken no notice of my
letter.  This is in my own handwriting, and for no
other paper. On Monday, January 28th, after the
second performnace of ‘ The Playboy,” one of the direc-
tors wrote to me that there was a fear that the theatre
might be damaged. In the ‘ Evening Telegraph’ for
January 29th, under the heading, ‘ Mr. Fay’s Speech,’
you will find this amiable remark about my property—
‘If you don’t pull the curtain -down, we will pull it
down for you.” There was a reference in a dialogue (T
fancy it was in THE LEADER) on the Abbey Theatre re-
opening in October about bringing in the police on the
first night of ‘ Mineral Workers;’ unfortunately it is
not in my cutting book. I heard of the occurrence
when abroad (by letter), and I felt it necessary to write
and express my regret to the person concerned, that
anyone should be insulted in my theatre, and my hope
that the directors would maintain decent order in the
future. T am too true a Londoner to mind any re-
marks abeut my ‘ British and lack-humour soul.” T
enjoy your paper intensely when you make fun of us
all.  Your clever contributors give me and my friends
a great deal of amusement. I read out the loveliest
bits to ‘a certain weakly poet,” and he enjoys them in
a most healthy manner. Please show a kindly spirit,
and go on giving us this pleasure. The writer of  The
Ruined Abbey ’ prophesies that which I sincerely hope
will come to pass. Yes, I hope to live to see my little
theatre in the hands of the house-breakers, torn down
to make room for a stately home of the drama near ‘the
limpid Liffey.” The Abbey Theatre, whatever its mis-
deeds may be, certainly provides much good ‘copy ’ for
the press of Dublin; hence the press ought tosbe much
pleased with,—Yours sincerely, A. E. F. Horniman."”

‘We pointed out in our comments last week that Miss
Horpiman displayed a lack of humour in that she
allowed herself to become quite solemn and serious con-
cerning the remark which “a voice,”’ or, as she put it,
the “audience,”’ made. The remark was “kill the
author.”” As we pointed out, that “ voice ’’ was rather
witty in view of the plot of the play, though, in our
opinion, it was not equal to the other “voice’ which
suggested to a noisy champion of freedom of judg-
ment”’ to “go home and kill your father.”
In her letter this week Miss Horniman’s lack of a sense
of humour is not so manifest; indeed, if we may ven-
ture to do so, we would like to congratulate her on the
gallant effort she makes to live down our comment. Last
week we said—“Certainly this is the first we ever
heard of any threat to pull down the curtain.”” The
remark that Miss Horniman quotes from the ¢ Tele-
graph’s ’ report is one that we should rather take in the
““ go-home-and-kill-your-father ** sense; however, we
admit that so “true a Londoner’’ may be excused for
taking it seriously. Probably if a play representing
London life, having for its main incident the cremating
of babies in a baby-farm, were staged within sound of
Bow Bells, the “true Londoners” might pull down the
curtain without even having the amiability to announce

their intention beforehand. The dialogue referred to

must have appeared in some other paper, for we have no

- recollection of it, and failed to find it on searching our
ﬁle.A The first information we received of the police
having been brought into the theatre in October was
contained in Miss Horniman’s previous letter. We did
hear reports of some most unseemly conduct on the part
of members of the audience one night which took the
form of a demonstration against Mrs. Gonne-M‘Bride,
but we heard nothing of the police having been called
in. Of course, this unseemly conduct had nothing to
do with the play that had been produced, nor with the
theatre; and conduct of that sort would be condemned
by-every person with a spark of decent feeling; it was
most . un-Irish, almost as un-Irish as “The Playboy.’’
We are glad that “a certain weekly paper’’ gives Miss
Horniman and her friends amusement ; we always aim
at being as amusing as the serious nature of our work
allows; but we trust that Miss Horniman gets more
than amusement from our columns, for we also purport
to retail enlightenment and instruction. We are not
surprised that “a certain weakly poet’’ enjoys our
humour. Miss Horniman may remember that in our
comments on “The Shadowy Waters’’ we suggested
that the author may after all be a comedian! There
are more “Playboys’’ than one associated with the
Abbey Theatre.

The civilians and constabulary of Boyle gave a return
quadrille party to their military friends recently! The
affair is described in a local paper as “a brilliant func-
tion.””  The decorations, if we can believe the local
reporters, ““could not but impress the most fastidious
critic of the decorative art.”’ Evidently the police of
Boyle can do something besides catch trout and play
concertinas.  The reporter goes on:—“ But who was
responsible for this? Certainly the great part of the
work fell to that popular and sturdy member of the Con-
stabulary, Constable Goff, who found ready seconders
in his laudable and creditable work in Constables Hear,
Dolan, and M‘Garry.”’

Some mere civilians lent a hand to the Constabulary,
and “these willing co-operators in the perfection of the
preliminaries had the invaluable advice and assistance
of Quarter Master Sergeant George Barnes, and the
fact of his name being associated with any such fixture
is sufficient to ensure that success will follow.””  But
even with Quarter Master Sergeant George Barnes the
“brilliant function ’’ would lack some of its sparkle “if

_ the services of that most genial of good fellows, Sergeant
Cullinane, were not available.”” Tt is not stated what
were the particular kind of services rendered by the Ser-
geant. We trust he did not have to act as  chucker-
out.”’

It is all very well to talk about the dulness of country
life in Ireland, but when a position with a salary
attached is to be filled, things are often lively enough.
The Ballinrobe District Council had recently to elect a
doctor to the No. 1 Dispensary District. The crowd, we
are told, attended to support one of the three candidates
who was a native of the town. We read that “the
boardroom was thronged almost to suffocation by a
crowd of persons who, judging from their demeanour,
were determined to eventually create a disturbance
which' would not be easily quelled. When the list - f
works and other matters on the agenda were got through
there appeared outside the room a crowd of about three
hundred persons with band and banner bearing the in-
scription, ¢ Vote for Corcoran.” The crowd for many
minutes indulged in cheering and booing the supporters
of the other candidates. The approaches of the work-
house were spanned with arches bearing mottoes such
‘as the foregoing. At 12 o’clock it was impossible to

gain admission to the hall, the people being packed like
" herrings in a barrel. When some of the guardians and
clergy arrived so dense was the crowd that neither
tardians, clergy, nor reporters could gain admission.
ing from the attitude of the crowd it was evident
he voters were undesirable. After much diffi-

Out of a total of 39 guardians 37 were present. One

some questions in Irish to Dr. Corcoran when one of the

Ono might inagine that it was in the Abbey Theatre
that the election was proceeding, for Mr. Joyce said,
“We must send for the police if we want to go on with
tho election.” Mr. Walsh asked Mr. Costello, “ Are
you one of the Finegans who took out their eye-balls
and put them in again?’ and Mr. Costello retorted on
Mr. Walsh, “ Are you one of the Hooligans?’  Mr.
Walsh said that Dr. Corcoran should be elected, as he
was the son of a “tenant farmer whose forefathers, for
three hundred years, paid rates in the Union.”” Even-
tually Dr. Corcoran was elected on a second poll by 18
votes to 14. Several people threw up their hats with
delight, and Mr. John Walsh, D.C., jumped on the
hoardroom table, and, waving his hat, shouted—“T
engineered it all!”’

They do other things besides promote temperance at
the Temperance Hall, Scariff, Co. Clare. - An embroi-
dery school is now established there. The promoters
of this cottage industry have secured the services of a
competent teacher, and they say:— We hope that: the
sale of the work may do a little to supplemeni the
otherwise scanty.earnings of many in our midst, and
thus, perhaps, help in some small way to stay the tide
of emigration, which all must deplore.  So far, our
efforts have not been subsidised from any quarter, and
for the considerable expenses, which are unavoidable
at the beginning, we must rely on the fees of pupils,
and the subscriptions of friends who sympathise with
our movement.””  We are informed that there are
already about fifty girls working at this industry, and
the promoters hope shortly to have some of the work on
the market ; they naturally hope for help from, amongst
others, the children of the “ Banner County > who live
in other lands. A ‘Clareman abroad, who buys a piece
of Clare-made embroidery, does, to say the least, as
patriotic a work as one who reads four or five columns
of a “great speech.” Contributions, and we assume,
orders, may be sent to the Hon. Sec. Embroidery School,
Temperance Hall, Scariff, Co. Clare.

Mr. Thomas Sexton, speaking at the annual meet-
ing of the “Simply Deplorable,”” said :—‘May I say
in my belief the shareholders of this company have
never been eager for large profits. So far as I know

them, and I  think I know them intimately,
their governing desire is that their journal
should serve the country well (applause). And that

desire being satisfied, as I'hope it has been satisfied.
they are not exacting in any other way (hear, hear).””
That sort of tall talk will scarcely go down. If divi-
dends are a secondary matter, why.does the “Freeman’’
continue to advertise such things as “The Spring
Chicken,”” ‘and “Kitty Grey?’ Ts the advertising of
these things a necessary part of the policy to “serve the
country well?”’ ;

Father McCann, P.P: of Ring, certainly has the

courage of his views on Irish-speaking people—and very

strange some of them are, What a curious stroke,

fortune placed him in an Trish-speaking parish, of all

places in the, world. At a meeting of the County.
Waterford Joint Committee,. of* Technical® Instru ;

_and Agriculture, Mr. Thomas Power proposed that £5
be ‘given towards the expenses of a local exhibition
which the Gaelic League purpose holding.  Canon
Power strongly supported the proposal, Father McCann,
P.P. of Ring, would not hear of it. According to a
newspaper report, he remarked:—“They (the com-
mittee) were entrusted with public funds, and he did
not see the use of wasting this money by giving it over
to some Dublin committee to do-as they wished with it.
He would be very sorry to contribute one shilling to the
Gaelic League. =~ Wherever they (the Gaelic League)
went, wherever there was a purely Irish-speaking dis-
brict, there was filth, squalor, ignorance and retro-
gression. The people of the district were more like
Orientals than anything else.”” This is nothing if not
breezy; indeed, as an onslaught on the Irish people
there has been nothing to equal it since the appearancoe
of the “ comedy > about the man who “killed his da.”’

Further on in the proceedings, Father McCann said :
“They are now encouraging free thought.”” To which
the chairman replied: “That is the  Sinn Fein’ policy.
The Gaelic League policy and the ‘Sinn Fein’ policy
are not' identical.”” We leave Mr. Edward Martyn and
Mr. John Swestman to deal with this reflection on the
“Sinn Fein’’ Party.

Father McCann is certainly an out-and-out opponent
of the Irish language, though we believe he is an Irish
speaker himself. He said :—“ Whencver he saw a farm
badly kept, covered with weeds and furze, and the
farm house partly tumbled down, he always found that
an Irish-speaking man lived there. The Gaelic League,
though their members were frequently there, never did
anything to try and elevate the minds of the people.
They never tried to better their condition.”” As Father
McCann is an Irish speaker himself, his own residence
ought to be in a shocking condition, as well as the Irish-
speaking part of his parish.

The difficulty of finding agreement on plain matters
of fact under the eyes of cbservers has for years im-
pressed us with the uncertainty of all accounts of past
history. Canon Power, who has eyes as well as Father
McCann, and whose capacity for cbservation we Lave no
reason to suppose is in any way inferior, looks out before
him, and here is what.-he sees:— Canon Power said
as a rule he found the houses and farms of Jrish-speak-
ing families the best kept of any. He found, too, that
the housckeepers of these families were the very best

around.” The proposed grant of £5 was passed by.

seven votes to three.

Father M‘Cann’s reference to “free thought’’ indi-
cates how, as a matter of fact, various matters with
which individual Gaelic Leaguers have been connected
will be used by those who do not like the movement.
The Portarlington affair, we believe, did a lot of
damage to the League, and the nagging at the school
managerial question by some Gaelic Leaguers has not
improved matters. All these things obviously give an
excuse not only to clergymen, but to Catholic laymen
who may want an excuse for not throwing themselves
into the language movement.  Sir Horace Plunkett
naturally wanted the good-will of the priests and the

- active co-operation of as many of them as possible to

THE LEADER. 3

help to push on his schemes.. He was getting both: to
a great extent,-and then he turned round and wrote his
book! Tt is more desirable for the Irish language move-
ment to have the good-will and co-operation of the
priests; as a matter of fact, if you took the priests who
have been pioneers and workers in the language move-
ment away, you would take a great slice off the move-
ment, probably the vital slice. As a body, the priests
have ‘done far more for the Irish language than any
other distinet body—the lawyers, doctors, business-men,
even the civil servants, who probably, as a class, .come
next to the Catholic clergy in the matter of work:done
for the Irish language. It is well to remember that dur-
ing-all the Land League agitations, during all the time
of Parnell and long, long afterwards, during the greater
part of the “Split,”” the Nationalists practically
ignored the language. If the language was not taught
in the schools, the necessity for its being taught was
not preached from the political platforms, nor advo-
cated in the political national press. The priests are edu-
cated Irishmen, so are the medical doctors and the soli-
citors. We see no reason why it should be expected that
the proportion of priests converted to the practically
new doctrine of Irish Ireland, a doctrine that was never
heard of under Parnell’s regime, should be greater than
the proportion amongst solicitors and medical doctors.
Yebt we believe that the priests have espoused the cause
of Irish Ireland far and away beyond any other distinct
class.  There are, of course, many priests as well ‘as
laymen who do not believe in the language movement ;
there are others on“the border line who, if all ‘went
well, could not see fheir way to keep out, but who
would be glad of an excuse for keeping out. Sir Horace
Plunkett’s book did not help the work with which he
is associated.

We have received another cheque from our reverend
friend who had already sent us five pounds requesting
that we should buy the Irish-Ireland nuc'eus of a parish
library with the money. The second cheque is for £10.
Our reverend friend writes:—“I am sending you a
further sum of £10 for the purchase of the °general
literature * and religious works referred to in my last
etter. Perhaps the best division would be £7 for the
former, and £3 for the latter. I trust you will be able
to complete the selection by Easter. The teachers have
kindly promised to superintend the distribution and
collection of the books. I intend to divide the lot
etween the three schools, changing them in rotation
every six months.”

It is very difficult to select suitable religious works.
I know a few good works, viz. :—Faith of our Fathers

We. have received the following letter :— Sir,—I
am at the present moment engaged in writing a play
for the National Theatre in Dublin. The principal
scene portrays a number of Trinity College students
who look callously on while a young woman, whom they
could easily rescue, is drowning. A friend of mine
declares that the Committee would consider this scene
a libel, and will, consequently, not accept the play, and
that if they did the University students would take
effectual steps to prevent it being properly presented
before a Dublin audience. I am loath to believe that
any body of Irishmen would allow such vulgar preju-
dices to interfere with their artistic perceptions; but I
should like your opinion on the point before proceeding
any further with my masterpiece.—I am, Sir, yours
faithfully, Shakespeare Dante Jones.”’:

i We certainly see no reason why Mr. S. D. Jones
[ - should not proceed with his masterpiece, but as a mat-
L. : ter -of prudence we would suggest that he should, at
< s an early date, communicate with the police. If Mr.
Jones is a novice in the art of requisitioning the ser-
vices of the poilce, we feel sure that the author of a
play called “Cathleen Ni Houlihan’’ would be happy
to give him any information he required.

Our readers will be pleased to hear of the continued
progress of the enterprising Dripsey Woollen Mills, Co.
Cork. At about the middle of February of this year
the sales for 1907 were equal to those during the whole
first quarter of 1906. The management have arranged
. - for another batch of new looms to be put in in April
next.

The Executive Committee of the Hotel and Tourist a4
Association are up in arms at “ Humphrey’s Dump.”
Humphrey’s Dump have given away the catering cou-
tract to a British firm, and this notwithstanding that
some of the hotel proprietors in Dublin are guarantors.
However, now that the guarantees have been given, the 3
Dump feels strong enough to put out its tongue at the e
caterers, and all the caterers can do is pass a resolution! e
A lot “ Humphrey’s Dump,”’ that dumped in the face kB
of a large volume of public opinion, cares for a resolu- Sl
tion! The resolution of the Executive Committee of
the Hotel and Tourist Association, runs as follows:— b
“ That this meeting of the Executive Committee of the
Hotel and Tourist Association (Ireland) has heard with
deep regret that the Finance and General Purposes Com-
mittee of the Irish International Exhibition has seen
fit to reject the tenders submitted by Irish catering
firms, and also to ignore the definite recommendation of
their own expert committee, the Catering Committee cf
the Exhibition, by placing the whole contract with a
London firm upon terms which were denied to Irish
firms, whereby a sum of £50,000 or £60,000 will be lost
to this country, and probably to the guarantors cf the
Exhibition, and we desire to place on record its strong
protest against the obvious unfairness of such a pro-
ceeding.”’

TR

Arising out of the resolution passed by the Executive
Committee of the Hotel and Tourist Association, we see
that Mr. Shanks, the Chief Executive Officer,’”’ of
Humphrey’s Dump, has rushed into print. Mr. James
Moran, of Talbot Street, who was on the Catering Com-
mitbee of the Dump, has also published correspondence.
e has resigned membership of the Catering Committee,
and promises that he intends, at “the earliest possible

The law was put in motion to take certain Catholic
children from Dr. Barnardo’s Homes some years ago, a..ud
the late Dr. Barnardo retorted that this was a dog-in-
the-manger policy on the part of Catholics, who did not,
want him to rescue children whom Catholics would not,
or could not, rescue themselves. Whether the taunt
was deserved or not, we cannot say. At the preseut
time we believe that the late Dr. Barnardo’s Committee
hand over to the Catholic authorities any Catholic child
for whom provision can be made in a Catholic Rescue
Home. During recent years we understand every
Catholic child so offered has been taken over by the
Catholic authorities. But things have come to a crisis .
in the affairs of the Incorporated Society of the Crusade
of Rescue and Home for Destitute Catholic Children.
The Society is in desperate financial straits. This is a
Society in England, and in the ordinary way Ireland
has more than enough to do to look after her own poor,
and she might well let English Catholics look after theirs.
But the so-called English Catholic poor are, unfortu-
nately, largely of Irish birth or extraction. Of the
820 children concerned the majority are Irish. . In
another part of the paper particulars are set out at
length, and our readers can judge for themselves con-
cerning the pressing needs of this charity with the for-
tunes, or misfortunes, of which the fate of so many Irish
children are bound up. The cry of the helpless waifs
of the exiles will soften many a kind heart amongst our
Irish readers, and it will appeal with an extra poignancy
to our readers who are in exile themselves in various
parts of Britain.

THE BRYCE SCHEME

HE Government’s University proposals seem, on the
whole, to have secured the support of the Catho-
lic laity, Mr. John Redmond’s declaration in favour of’ 3
them’ being the most remarkable. The two }mportant
bodies who have not yet expressed an opinion about
tliem are, on the one hand, the Hierarchy, and on the
other, the Presbyterians of the North. It is, indeed,
an open secret that most of the bishops would as educa-
tionalists have preferred a Royal University solution,.
but it does not scem likely that they will on this ground
reject the Government’s proposals. ' As to the Presby-
terians, howevor, their attitude cannot be predicted
with any certainty, and I should not be surprised to
seo them at any moment nail their colours to the Royal
University mast; for it is with them' alone that ‘the
Royal University has all along found fayour.

In any settlement of the University Question at pre-
sent, the choice really lies between two schemes, both
in themselves bad, a reformed Royal University,
with Trinity as one of its colleges (miscalled “Dublin
University *’ in the present instance), and a reformed
Royal University having no connection with Trinity
College. English prejudice makes four separate Univer-
sities impossible, since one of them must be a Univer-
sity for Catholics. ~ Whilst the mere addition of a
second college to Dublin University would leave the
Belfast Presbyterians out in the cold; and a Liberal
Government is not going to do that. Of the two pos-
sible schemes, the former, which makes Trinity a
college of the R.U.IL., is inexpressibly galling to the
graduates of that University. 'The only real argument
in its favour from a Catholic point of view is that in
the common examinations the prestige of Trinity is
likely to be shown up. The other arguments as to
bringing the young men of Ireland together, etc., are
more or less nonsense. For years to come nothing of
the sort is likely to happen, except in the Abbey
Theatre or a police-cell. ~ Nor will the new colleges
share in the prestige of Dublin University, save in a
very faint degree. Trinity College  is Trinity College
whatever you call it. For the alternative solution,
the Royal University, pure and simple, the standard
argument is that the R.U.I. is a University of a
“more modern type’’ than T.C.D. But this line of
reasoning is in its turn a little thin. The classical
examiners in the R.U.I. ask very much the same emen-
dations and other absurdities of their candidates as
their brethren of Dublin University. A young man
coming from one University is, the day he gets his
degree, quite as unfit to earn a living as one coming
from the other.

There is really very little to be said for either the
Government, scheme - or its Royal University alterna-
tive. They are both uneducational political expe-
dients. The worst thing about the Bryce scheme is that
if, involves another University smash—the bankruptcy
of the R.U.I., just as the Queen’s University put up
its shutters in 1879—with the moral certainty of a third,
the dissolution of the new Dublin University within
twenty years. These sudden insolvencies hit very hard’
the man who has invested his little capital of energy
in an Irish degrec. 'He can ab’ best solace. himself
with the morose delectation to be derived from witness-
ing the speechless indignation of Trinity Protestants
and Trinity Catholics for that matter. Still we have
refused so much, and got, so little, on the University
Question that though I dislike the new scheme, T, for
one, certainly should not, think of opposing it.

How much will Trinity College opposition be able to
offect? They have litfle weight with the Government ;
but will, of course, have the full support of the Opposi-

little amount of local self-government wrung from Eng-

TRINITY ON TOUR.

"I" RINITY is in a tight place; of that there can be
no doubt at all. It is at once in a red rage and
a blue funk at the awful prospect of being made a col-
lege of the Royal University. ~ Why did Mr. Bryce
make that famous speech at a time when he was practi- -
cally no longer Chief Secretary? Why, indeed, except
that the scheme is evidently his, and he wants the
world to be aware of the one thing he did, or at all
events projected, during his one year of office in Ire-
land. Thoughtless people had doubtless sized up the
Right Hon. James B. as a mere doctrinaire ex-professor,
but the world has now to recognise him as a joker of
the first rank, for surely a better joke than that of mak-
ing Trinity a college of the Royal University was never
thought of. Anyhow, as a practical joke, it is
unrivalled. But Trinity has not yet seen the fun, and
is taking the thing most seriously. Let us study the
manceuvres of Trinity under this dread visitation.

The tactics of Trinity under this awful ordeal have
not been wanting in promptitude and inventiveness, but
they have been a little too thin. Trinity adopted the
“stage army ’’ mode of demonstration, a resource effec-
tive enough where. illusions are taken for granted, but
of dubious efficacy in the eyes of the initiated.  First
of all, a meeting of; the Fellows, Professors, Lecturers,
etc., was held to. condemn the Bryce scheme. Next
came. a meebing of the Senate, for the same purpose—
the same stage army coming on again, with just a few
additional “supers’ to save appearances; almost all
the speakers belonged to the stage army already men-
tioned. Next came a meeting of the “ Council *’ (what-
ever that is), and: again the “stage army’ filed in,
determined to do,or die in defence of old Trinity. Thus
we have had in all three separate appearances of the
stage army which swallows up so. great a part of the
ninety thousand a year that Trinity soaks into herself.
And now a select company of the stage army has gone,
so to speak, on foreign service.

The. object of the Trinity flying column is to work up
feeling in fayour of “this, ancienf seat of learning’
amongst all whose hearts can, by any possibility, be
moved, or even supposed likely to be moved. For in-
stance, Mr. Birrell, our new Chief Secretary, is one of
those upon whom the deputation have called ; Mr. Bal-
four, of course, is another. The deputation also visits
Oxford and Cambridge. The flying column of the stage
army is made of Tony, the Provost; the Rev. T. T.
Gray, Mr. Beare, Mr. Edward Gwynun, Sir Charles
Ball and Dr. Joly, the two latter representing the Pro-
fessors. The name of the great Mahaffy is eminent by
its absence—and has been all through. How be this?
Is the pass sold? Is John Pentland going to leave
alma maler to her doom? Looks like it. By the way,
the Rev. T. T. Gray is the representative of the Senior
